Computational reproduction & adversarial validation report

Mapping — and Stress-Testing — the SARS-CoV-2 Host Interactome

A live-data reproduction of Gordon et al.'s host-directed drug-repurposing map, extended with pathway enrichment and guilt-by-association prediction, then subjected to fourteen adversarial tests — including checks against an independent lab's dataset, an independent curated network, and an unbiased Y2H interactome — to find out which claims actually survive.

Source study Gordon et al., Nature 583:459–468 (2020), doi:10.1038/s41586-020-2286-9 Interactions analyzed 332 Viral proteins 27 Validation tests 14

Abstract

Gordon et al. (2020) used affinity-purification mass spectrometry to identify 332 high-confidence physical interactions between 27 SARS-CoV-2 viral proteins and human host proteins, then mapped 69 of those host proteins onto existing drugs as host-directed antiviral candidates. This report pulls that same interaction set live from its public NDEx record and layers three additional analyses on top: (1) pathway enrichment (GO/KEGG/Reactome via Enrichr) to characterize which biological processes each viral protein hijacks, (2) guilt-by-association network expansion (STRING) to predict host proteins that plausibly interact with a given viral protein but were not detected in the original screen, and (3) a fourteen-part adversarial validation suite built specifically to find out whether (2) produces real signal or database artifacts.

The enrichment step recovers biology consistent with the original paper — centrosome and mitotic pathways concentrated on Nsp13, DNA-replication pathways on Nsp1, mitochondrial-import pathways on Nsp4/Orf9b/Orf9c — though a coverage audit shows Reactome annotates only 68.7% of the 332 proteins, unevenly across viral proteins, so cross-bait comparisons are not on equal footing. The prediction step produced two headline candidates: PLK1 as a missing Nsp13 interactor, and TOMM70 as a missing Nsp4 interactor. Validation shows an important asymmetry: the Nsp13–PLK1 call is specific (not a generic network hub; 0/25 hits under a degree-preserving null), reasonably robust across a parameter sweep, and has above-null but modest predictive value overall (11.1% recall / 3.5% precision on held-out real interactions, versus 0%/0% for the null) — but it has not been independently confirmed in any other published SARS-CoV-2 interactome. The Nsp4–TOMM70 call, originally framed as cross-validated, does not survive scrutiny as well: it is threshold-fragile (present only at the loosest STRING confidence setting) and every independent confirmation of TOMM70 as a real SARS-CoV-2 target (seven papers) implicates ORF9b, never Nsp4. The most consequential finding came from following those next steps. Re-running the prediction against a genuinely independent dataset (Stukalov et al. 2021, a separate lab and pipeline) as the answer key — rather than an internal split of Gordon's own data — collapsed mean recall from 11.1% to 0.07%, indistinguishable from a matched null. A follow-up diagnosis shows why: the method only works where a bait's interactors already form a tight STRING cluster, and that clustering is driven by literature co-mention (text-mining channel, rho +0.78) rather than hard experimental evidence (rho +0.50). In plain terms, the method predicts well between already-famous proteins and fails on novel biology. Two closing tests split the headline calls apart. Rebuilt on IntAct's curated physical-interaction graph — a structurally different network with no text-mining channel — Nsp13–PLK1 reproduces (6 of 40 Nsp13 preys are direct physical partners of PLK1, p=0.0005; PLK1 in the top ~1.2% of candidates, among other centrosome proteins), so it is not a STRING artifact; Nsp4–TOMM70 does not (0/8, unranked) and is withdrawn. The bottom line: Nsp13–PLK1 is a specific, cross-method-reproducible hypothesis about the machinery Nsp13 engages, still awaiting a direct binding assay; Nsp4–TOMM70 does not survive; and the prediction method as a whole should be treated as hypothesis-generating, not confirmatory. A final search for a bias-free confirmation substrate (HuRI, an unbiased systematic-Y2H interactome) maps the ceiling of what computation can settle here: HuRI is structurally blind to the centrosome (10 of 14 core proteins absent, PLK1 at degree 1), so no unbiased binary dataset can adjudicate Nsp13–PLK1 — every dataset that covers this biology is literature-curated. Confirming it now strictly requires a wet-lab co-IP, and saying so plainly is more useful than manufacturing closure.

Full validation ledger

All fourteen adversarial tests, and what each one actually checks

Tests 1–8 probe the two specific headline predictions; tests 9–13 step back and ask whether the prediction method itself has real predictive value against independent data and an independently-built network. This table is the complete underlying evidence.

TestVerdictWhat it found
1. Identifier integrity Pass 331/332 prey gene symbols exactly match their UniProt accession's official HGNC name; the 1 exception (SLC9A3R1/NHERF1) is a legitimate synonym, not an error. Zero broken joins, zero duplicate accessions.
2. Negative controls Pass Ran the identical prediction procedure on E, M, and Orf7a (baits with no centrosome/mitochondrial biology). PLK1 and TOMM70 never appear; predictions stay topically anchored (bromodomain proteins for E, ribosome-biogenesis factors for Orf7a).
3. Coverage / denominator audit Concern confirmed Reactome annotates only 68.7% of the 332 proteins (KEGG: 46.4%); coverage is uneven across viral proteins, so Figure 2's blank columns partly reflect database gaps, not biology.
4. Circularity strip Pass Removing the 5 SARS/COVID-named Reactome terms doesn't reorder the remaining ranking (Figure 5).
5. Threshold robustness Split verdict Nsp13–PLK1 survives 5/6 confidence settings and 3/4 candidate-window widths. Nsp4–TOMM70 survives only the single loosest confidence setting (0.4) — the fishing signature this test exists to catch.
6. Degree-preserving null model Pass 0 of 25 randomized same-size gene sets produced PLK1; 0 of 25 produced TOMM70. Neither is a generic STRING super-hub that any gene set would attract.
7. Held-out precision / recall Real but uneven 11.1% mean recall / 3.5% mean precision on real bait labels vs. 0%/0% for the degree-matched null (50 trials each) — genuine, above-chance signal, but recall ranges from 46.7% (N) and 27.5% (Nsp13) down to 0% (M, Nsp7, Nsp12, Orf9b).
8. External validation (IntAct) Not confirmed Neither predicted edge appears in any independent SARS-CoV-2 interactome. TOMM70's 7 independent confirmations are all with ORF9b; PLK1's 1 independent record is with Spike. Guilt-by-association validated only by more association is a closed loop — this is the one test that could have actually confirmed a prediction, and it didn't, for either.
9. Independent AP-MS crosscheck (Stukalov et al. 2021) Not supported (low power) Pulled Stukalov et al.'s own published hit list directly from Nature's supplementary files. Their SARS-CoV-2 Nsp13 has exactly one significant interactor (RNF8), not PLK1. But Nsp13 was the single sparsest bait in their whole 22-bait panel — their assay barely recovered anything for it (and its one hit doesn't overlap Gordon's 40-protein list either), so this is a weak miss, not a refutation.
10. Precision/recall vs. independent ground truth Method fails to generalize The decisive test. Using Gordon's full prey lists to predict what Stukalov independently found (18 baits): mean recall 0.07% (real) vs. 0.02% (null) — both near zero. The lone exception, Orf3a, recovered 4/329 novel Stukalov interactors (VPS16/18/41/45, all HOPS complex — matching real ORF3a-autophagy biology), and is the only bait with enough independent data to be well-powered. The internal 11.1% recall (test 7) was optimistic; against truly external biology the method mostly performs at chance.
11. Recall-gap diagnosis Success is literature-biased Explains test 10 (Figure 6). Holdout recall tracks how densely a bait's preys are already interconnected in STRING (rho +0.65), and that density is driven by the text-mining channel (rho +0.78) more than hard experimental evidence (rho +0.50). The method predicts well between already-famous proteins and fails on novel ones — which is why it looked good internally and collapsed against independent data.
12. Alternative-substrate reproduction (IntAct) PLK1 reproduces, TOMM70 dies Redid the prediction on IntAct's curated physical-interaction graph (no text-mining channel — Figure 7). Nsp13–PLK1 reproduces: 6/40 preys are direct physical partners of PLK1 (all centrosome/centriole), vs. null mean 1.3, p=0.0005; PLK1 in the top ~1.2% of candidates. First positive cross-method evidence — not a STRING artifact. Nsp4–TOMM70 does not: 0/8, unranked. Caveat: IntAct is also literature-curated, so it shares some study bias.
13. Broadened pathway coverage Gap is structural Adding WikiPathways 2023, BioPlanet 2019, and MSigDB Hallmark 2020 lifts union coverage of the 332 genes only from 95.2% to 96.4%; 12 of the 16 genes unannotated in GO/KEGG/Reactome stay dark across all six libraries, and the same membrane/small-ORF baits stay coverage-poor. The enrichment blind spots (Figure 2) are genuinely under-characterized biology, not one database's quirk.
14. Non-curated binary interactome (HuRI) Confirmation ceiling The reviewer asked for an unbiased dataset that could confirm PLK1, since STRING/IntAct/Gordon are all literature-curated. HuRI (systematic Y2H, not literature-assembled) is that dataset — but it is structurally blind to the biology (Figure 8): 10 of 14 core centrosome proteins are entirely absent, PLK1 has degree 1, and only 1 of the 6 IntAct preys supporting PLK1 even appears. A 0-overlap here is a Y2H coverage failure, not a refutation. The ceiling: every dataset that covers the centrosome is literature-curated, and the one unbiased binary map can't see it — so the study-bias caveat on Nsp13–PLK1 cannot be removed computationally. Only a wet-lab co-IP can.

Where this goes next

Next steps — all six executed

These were the six next steps identified during validation. All six have now been run; the first three produced the report's central negative result (the method doesn't generalize), and the second three resolved the two headline calls (PLK1 reproduces on an independent network; TOMM70 withdrawn) and hardened the pipeline. What remains is no longer computational bookkeeping but the experimental program below.

✓

Get an independent answer on Nsp13–PLK1  Done — not confirmed

Pulled Stukalov et al. 2021's own published AP-MS hit list directly (ledger test 9). Their Nsp13 has exactly one significant interactor, RNF8 — not PLK1. The miss is low-power (Nsp13 was their sparsest bait), so it weakens rather than refutes the hypothesis. A wet-lab co-IP remains the only definitive test.

✓

Re-run precision/recall against genuinely independent ground truth  Done — method fails to generalize

Executed (ledger test 10): using Stukalov et al.'s independent interactions as the answer key, mean recall fell to 0.07%, indistinguishable from a 0.02% null across 18 baits. The internal 11.1% was optimistic. Only Orf3a showed real above-null signal (4/329, the HOPS complex), and it's the one bait with enough independent data to be well-powered.

✓

Diagnose the bait-dependent recall gap  Done — literature bias

Executed (ledger test 11, Figure 6). Recall tracks how densely a bait's preys are already interconnected in STRING (rho +0.65), and that density is driven by literature co-mention (text-mining rho +0.78) more than hard evidence (rho +0.50). This is the mechanism behind the test-10 failure: the method rewards fame, not evidence.

✓

Broaden pathway coverage before trusting the ranking further  Done — gap is structural

Executed (ledger test 13): adding WikiPathways, BioPlanet, and MSigDB Hallmark lifts union coverage of the 332 genes only 95.2%→96.4%; 12 of the 16 unannotated genes stay dark across all six libraries. The blind spots are genuinely under-characterized biology, not one database's quirk.

✓

Try a second, structurally different guilt-by-association method  Done — PLK1 reproduces

Executed twice (ledger tests 12, 14): on IntAct's curated physical graph Nsp13–PLK1 reproduces (p=0.0005, Figure 7); on the unbiased HuRI Y2H map the centrosome is uncovered so the test is uninformative (Figure 8). The reproduction on a structurally different network is the strongest computational support the hypothesis has.

✓

Make the pre-flight checks automatic  Done

Done: preflight.py runs the identifier-integrity and coverage checks before enrichment/prediction and exits non-zero on a broken join, malformed accession, or near-zero library coverage — verified to fail loudly on an injected bad accession.

Beyond the ceiling

The experimental program: what the surviving hypothesis becomes

Computation reached its ceiling for Nsp13→PLK1 (test 14). The follow-up splits into a wet-lab experiment and two bias-free computational lines that don't query interaction databases at all — and together they have turned "Nsp13 binds PLK1" into a precise, mechanism-specific, testable model.

Motif analysis reframed the hypothesis

Nsp13 carries no canonical PLK1 Polo-box docking motif (and only background-rate kinase-consensus sites). That argues against a direct kinase–substrate contact and for a bridged model: Nsp13 engages the PLK1-regulated centrosome through the CEP scaffolds it directly binds, rather than PLK1 itself.

The deciding experiments (details in the repository's experimental/ folder)

A reciprocal co-immunoprecipitation with the confirmed centrosome preys as internal positive controls and an explicit direct-vs-bridged disambiguation (in-vitro reconstitution + CEP knockdown); and an AlphaFold-Multimer run — a 7-job matrix with domain-split tests, positive/negative/bridge controls, and a structural null — the one remaining computational avenue that is both bias-free and able to give positive evidence, now designed to localize the bridged-vs-direct interface the motif and functional-genomics lines pointed to.

Reproducing this report

Data sources and how to rerun everything

Every figure and number above comes from live public data sources queried at run time, not from a static snapshot:

  • NDEx — network HEK293T_SARS-CoV-2, UUID 43803262-6d69-11ea-bfdc-0ac135e8bacf — the real 332 interactions.
  • Enrichr (via gseapy) — GO Biological Process, KEGG, Reactome libraries — pathway enrichment and coverage audit.
  • STRING REST API — network-expansion guilt-by-association scoring, threshold sweeps, null model, holdout tests, and the per-channel density diagnosis (Figure 6).
  • UniProt REST API — identifier integrity check.
  • IntAct via PSICQUIC (EBI) — independent external validation (test 8).
  • Springer/Nature static content — Stukalov et al. 2021 Supplementary Table 2, the independent AP-MS dataset used as ground truth in tests 9–10.
source .venv/bin/activate
python3 pipeline/fetch_network.py && python3 pipeline/enrichment.py && python3 pipeline/predict_missing.py
python3 pipeline/visualize.py && python3 pipeline/visualize_prediction.py
for f in validation/[0-9]*.py; do python3 "$f"; done

Full per-test methodology and honest caveats about the validation suite's own limits: validation/VALIDATION_REPORT.md. Project README: README.md.

Analysis and report generated against the Gordon et al. (2020) SARS-CoV-2–human interactome. Underlying study: Gordon, D.E. et al. Nature 583, 459–468 (2020). doi:10.1038/s41586-020-2286-9. All pathway, network, and validation figures in this report are reproducible from the scripts in this repository against live public data as of the analysis date.
